data.title: Is ON Semiconductor (ON) Fairly Priced After Recent Share Price Strength?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether ON Semiconductor is priced attractively right now, it helps to step back and look at how the current share price lines up with different valuation checks.
  • The stock last closed at US$72.21, with returns of 10.8% over 7 days, 19.2% over 30 days, 27.4% year to date, 39.3% over 1 year, an 11.8% decline over 3 years, and 72.5% over 5 years.
  • Recent price action has been shaped by ongoing investor interest in semiconductor names and how they might fit into themes such as electrification and advanced computing. ON Semiconductor has also been regularly covered in sector commentary that groups it with peers exposed to long term chip demand trends, which helps explain why sentiment has been active around the stock.
  • On our checks, ON Semiconductor currently has a valuation score of 2 out of 6. Next, we will walk through what different valuation approaches say about that score, and then finish with a perspective that may give you a deeper read on whether the current price suits your own investment style.

ON Semiconductor scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: ON Semiconductor Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s value to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth per share.

For ON Semiconductor, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.04b. Analyst and extrapolated projections used in the model show free cash flow figures in the coming years that reach $2.37b by 2030, with interim years such as 2026 and 2027 using estimates of $1.66b and $1.98b, respectively, before discounting.

Once all these projected cash flows are discounted back to today and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of around $68.89 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $72.21, this implies the stock is roughly 4.8% above the DCF estimate, which is a relatively small gap in valuation terms.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

ON Semiconductor is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for ON Semiconductor.

Approach 2: ON Semiconductor Price vs Sales

For a profitable company that is well covered by the market, the P/S ratio can be a useful cross check because it compares what investors are paying for each dollar of revenue, regardless of short term earnings swings.

In general, higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” or “fair” multiple, while slower expected growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower one. That is why simply looking at a single headline ratio rarely tells the full story.

ON Semiconductor currently trades on a P/S ratio of 4.75x. This sits below the Semiconductor industry average of 6.14x and the peer group average of 12.13x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for ON Semiconductor is 4.68x, which is an estimate of what the P/S might be based on factors such as its growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and key risks.

The Fair Ratio can give you a more tailored anchor than broad peer or industry comparisons because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming all chip stocks should trade on similar multiples. With the actual 4.75x P/S very close to the 4.68x Fair Ratio, the shares look broadly in line with this framework.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

P/S ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your ON Semiconductor Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. These are short, plain language stories that you and other investors create on Simply Wall St’s Community page to link a view of ON Semiconductor’s business to a set of forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and then to a Fair Value that you can compare with today’s price to decide whether the stock looks attractive or stretched. These Narratives update automatically as new news or earnings arrive so your view is never static. For example, one ON Semiconductor Narrative currently anchors on a Fair Value of about US$78.63, another uses US$63.00, and a more cautious one sits nearer US$44.08, showing how different assumptions about growth, profitability and required P/E can lead thoughtful investors to very different yet clearly explained valuation anchors.

For ON Semiconductor, here are previews of two leading ON Semiconductor Narratives:

🐂 ON Semiconductor Bull Case

Fair value anchor: US$78.63 per share

Gap to that fair value: current price of US$72.21 is about 8.2% below this narrative’s anchor

Revenue growth assumption: 10.08% a year

  • Argues that manufacturing changes, faster utilization recovery and higher margin segments could lift earnings power more than current expectations.
  • Sees ON Semiconductor as well placed in EVs, AI data centers and industrial sensing, supported by a broad manufacturing footprint.
  • Flags meaningful risks around China exposure, automotive cycles, execution in silicon carbide and Treo, competition and revenue lost from portfolio reshaping.

🐻 ON Semiconductor Bear Case

Fair value anchor: US$63.00 per share

Gap to that fair value: current price of US$72.21 is about 14.6% above this narrative’s anchor

Revenue growth assumption: 7.09% a year

  • Sees growth from EV, AI and power solutions and expects efficiency efforts to support margins and cash generation over time.
  • Highlights that progress depends on higher utilization, successful product mix upgrades and continued demand in key end markets.
  • Warns that cyclicality in autos, revenue headwinds from exiting legacy products, competitive pressure and geopolitical issues could limit earnings and valuation.

Together, these two narratives outline a range of possible outcomes that you can weigh against your own expectations for ON Semiconductor’s growth, risk and preferred price anchor.

data.title: Uno Minda's (NSE:UNOMINDA) 35% CAGR outpaced the company's earnings growth over the same five-year period
data.description:
For many, the main point of investing in the stock market is to achieve spectacular returns. And we've seen some truly amazing gains over the years. To wit, the Uno Minda Limited (NSE:UNOMINDA) share price has soared 340% over five years. If that doesn't get you thinking about long term investing, we don't know what will. It's even up 7.4% in the last week.

The past week has proven to be lucrative for Uno Minda investors, so let's see if fundamentals drove the company's five-year performance.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

There is no denying that markets are sometimes efficient, but prices do not always reflect underlying business performance. One way to examine how market sentiment has changed over time is to look at the interaction between a company's share price and its earnings per share (EPS).

During five years of share price growth, Uno Minda achieved compound earnings per share (EPS) growth of 68% per year. This EPS growth is higher than the 34% average annual increase in the share price. Therefore, it seems the market has become relatively pessimistic about the company. Of course, with a P/E ratio of 63.05, the market remains optimistic.

You can see below how EPS has changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

We know that Uno Minda has improved its bottom line lately, but is it going to grow revenue? This free report showing analyst revenue forecasts should help you figure out if the EPS growth can be sustained.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. Arguably, the TSR gives a more comprehensive picture of the return generated by a stock. In the case of Uno Minda, it has a TSR of 345% for the last 5 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. The dividends paid by the company have thusly boosted the total shareholder return.

A Different Perspective

It's nice to see that Uno Minda shareholders have received a total shareholder return of 38% over the last year. Of course, that includes the dividend. Since the one-year TSR is better than the five-year TSR (the latter coming in at 35% per year), it would seem that the stock's performance has improved in recent times. Someone with an optimistic perspective could view the recent improvement in TSR as indicating that the business itself is getting better with time. data.title: Shrinking TTM Loss At 10x Genomics (TXG) Tests Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

10x Genomics FY 2025 earnings snapshot

10x Genomics (TXG) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$166.0 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.13, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$642.8 million and a TTM EPS loss of US$0.35 that keeps the focus squarely on the income statement. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue range between US$149.0 million and US$172.9 million per quarter, with basic EPS swinging from a profit of US$0.28 in Q2 2025 to losses of US$0.28 in Q1, US$0.22 in Q3 and US$0.13 in Q4 as margins shifted through the year. This gives investors a mixed picture on earnings quality and cost control.

See our full analysis for 10x Genomics.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the widely held narratives about 10x Genomics. This highlights where the story around growth, losses and margins is confirmed and where it is being challenged.

See what the community is saying about 10x Genomics

TTM losses narrow to US$43.5 million

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, 10x Genomics recorded revenue of US$642.8 million and a net loss of US$43.5 million, compared with a TTM loss of US$182.6 million at FY 2024. This lines up with the comment that losses have been shrinking at about 28.7% per year over the last five years.
  • Bulls point out that this multi year improvement in losses, together with modest 4.4% TTM revenue growth, supports their view that the business model is maturing. Yet:
    • analysts in the balanced narrative still do not expect profitability within the next three years, even as they factor in revenue growth assumptions of 2.2% a year,
    • so the current TTM loss of US$43.5 million mainly supports the bullish argument about progress on the income statement, while also reminding you that the path to positive EPS is not reflected in the forecasts provided.

Investors who think this improving loss profile tells only half the story may want to see how bullish analysts connect it to their longer term view of the business. 🐂 10x Genomics Bull Case

Q2 profit stands out in a loss making year

  • Across FY 2025, three quarters were loss making with net losses of US$34.4 million in Q1, US$27.5 million in Q3 and US$16.3 million in Q4, while Q2 produced net income of US$34.5 million and basic EPS of US$0.28. This contrasts with the TTM EPS loss of US$0.35.
  • Bears argue that high costs and funding pressures limit earnings recovery, and this mix of one profitable quarter against three loss making ones fits that concern, but also complicates it:
    • the step down in quarterly losses from US$49.0 million in Q4 2024 to US$16.3 million in Q4 2025, alongside that profitable Q2, shows the income statement is not consistently weak across every period,
    • yet the risk summary still points to forecast earnings declining about 4% per year over the next three years, which lines up with the cautious view that recent profit in Q2 may not translate into a stable trend without clearer evidence.

If you are weighing that single profitable quarter against the longer stretch of losses, it can help to see how skeptics frame the downside case based on these numbers. 🐻 10x Genomics Bear Case

Slow 4.4% revenue growth and a richer P/S

  • Revenue over the last twelve months is described as growing at about 4.4% per year to US$642.8 million, while the shares trade on a P/S of 3.6x. This matches the peer average but sits above the US Life Sciences industry average of 2.8x.
  • The consensus narrative sees product breadth and international reach as supports for long term revenue streams, and the current data partly backs and partly tests that view:
    • on one hand, modest 4.4% revenue growth and continued losses mean the premium to the wider industry on P/S is being supported mainly by expectations about future product demand and global uptake, rather than current profitability,
    • on the other hand, the risk summary notes that revenue is forecast to grow slower than the broader US market at 10.3% a year and that earnings are expected to keep declining, so the valuation gap to the industry asks investors to judge whether the product and partnership story is strong enough to offset the slower growth profile in the data.

data.title: Dr. Reddy's Laboratories' (NSE:DRREDDY) investors will be pleased with their respectable 43% return over the last three years
data.description:
Investors can buy low cost index fund if they want to receive the average market return. But if you invest in individual stocks, some are likely to underperform. For example, the Dr. Reddy's Laboratories Limited (NSE:DRREDDY) share price return of 41% over three years lags the market return in the same period. Looking at more recent returns, the stock is up 5.9% in a year.

With that in mind, it's worth seeing if the company's underlying fundamentals have been the driver of long term performance, or if there are some discrepancies.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

There is no denying that markets are sometimes efficient, but prices do not always reflect underlying business performance. One flawed but reasonable way to assess how sentiment around a company has changed is to compare the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price.

During three years of share price growth, Dr. Reddy's Laboratories achieved compound earnings per share growth of 16% per year. The average annual share price increase of 12% is actually lower than the EPS growth. Therefore, it seems the market has moderated its expectations for growth, somewhat.

You can see how EPS has changed over time in the image below (click on the chart to see the exact values).

Before buying or selling a stock, we always recommend a close examination of historic growth trends, available here.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

As well as measuring the share price return, investors should also consider the total shareholder return (TSR). The TSR incorporates the value of any spin-offs or discounted capital raisings, along with any dividends, based on the assumption that the dividends are reinvested. Arguably, the TSR gives a more comprehensive picture of the return generated by a stock. In the case of Dr. Reddy's Laboratories, it has a TSR of 43% for the last 3 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. And there's no prize for guessing that the dividend payments largely explain the divergence!

A Different Perspective

Dr. Reddy's Laboratories shareholders are up 6.6% for the year (even including dividends). But that return falls short of the market. If we look back over five years, the returns are even better, coming in at 7% per year for five years. It's quite possible the business continues to execute with prowess, even as the share price gains are slowing. data.description:

  • The stock has seen mixed returns, with a 3.3% decline over the last 7 days, a 10.5% decline over the last 30 days, a 14.1% decline year to date, a 34.6% gain over the last year and a very large 3 year return of 215.5% alongside a 192.5% return over 5 years.
  • Recent price moves often tie back to changing views on insurance risk, regulatory developments, or company specific events, and HCI Group is no exception. While short term swings can feel noisy, they often raise useful questions about whether the current price still lines up with the underlying business.
  • On our simple valuation framework HCI Group scores 5 out of 6. This raises some clear questions about where the market might be mispricing the stock that we will test using several valuation approaches, then finish with a way of thinking about value that goes beyond the usual multiples and models.

HCI Group delivered 34.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Insurance industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: HCI Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that equity investors typically require, then capitalizes that stream of excess value into an intrinsic per share estimate.

For HCI Group, the model starts with a Book Value of $63.41 per share and a Stable EPS of $27.65 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The implied Cost of Equity is $6.40 per share, which leaves an Excess Return of $21.25 per share. That spread reflects an Average Return on Equity of 30.14% on a Stable Book Value base of $91.74 per share, using weighted future book value estimates from 2 analysts.

When those excess returns are projected and discounted, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $687.22 per share, compared with a recent share price around $157.96. On this framework, HCI Group screens as around 77.0% undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: HCI Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like HCI Group, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay today to the earnings the business is already generating. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when growth prospects are more modest or risks feel higher.

HCI Group currently trades on a P/E of about 10.36x. That sits below the Insurance industry average of 12.31x and well below the peer group average of 25.19x.
